stage.name: LSU map: where memory stops being a simple operand
stage.description: A source-grounded LSU map covering load and store result paths, commit interaction, D-cache requests, PMP inputs, and memory-side exceptions.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: The LSU is not just an address adder

content.title: LSU map: where memory stops being a simple operand
content.subtitle: Loads and stores look like ordinary instructions in assembly, but the backend treats them as memory protocol, ordering, exception, and commit problems.
content.thesis: Read the LSU as the place where execute-stage work meets memory-system reality and commit-stage discipline.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

content.speakerNotes: Unit 07 starts with a careful map. A load or store is not just an ALU add followed by magic memory. The CVA6 LSU interfaces with the data cache, reports load and store results or exceptions, connects to commit, and receives PMP configuration. In other words, memory is where arithmetic, ordering, protection, and side effects all show up at once.

actions.text: Unit 07 starts with a careful map. A load or store is not just an ALU add followed by magic memory. The CVA6 LSU interfaces with the data cache, reports load and store results or exceptions, connects to commit, and receives PMP configuration. In other words, memory is where arithmetic, ordering, protection, and side effects all show up at once.

scenes.title: Place the LSU inside the backend

content.title: LSU sits in execute, but stores become real through commit
content.body: Issue sends memory operations into the LSU. Loads can return values and exceptions to issue. Stores are tracked and require commit coordination before their memory side effect becomes non-speculative.
content.caption: Use the overview diagram to place LSU between issue, execute, commit, and the memory system.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: COMMIT_STAGE
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE updates architectural state, commits stores, manages exceptions, and exposes commit_lsu signals toward EX_STAGE.

content.speakerNotes: The first professional habit is to separate placement from authority. The LSU is part of the execute-side functional-unit map, but that does not mean every memory action is immediately architectural. Loads produce values and exceptions. Stores need the commit story we built in Unit 06. This is why memory is the natural next chapter after precise state.

actions.text: The first professional habit is to separate placement from authority. The LSU is part of the execute-side functional-unit map, but that does not mean every memory action is immediately architectural. Loads produce values and exceptions. Stores need the commit story we built in Unit 06. This is why memory is the natural next chapter after precise state.

scenes.title: Read the LSU block as four contracts

content.title: Four questions the LSU must answer
content.subtitle: The official block diagram is best read as contracts, not as a wall of signals.

cards.title: Can I accept it?
cards.body: lsu_ready and bypass capacity decide whether issue can send another memory operation.

cards.title: Is the address legal?
cards.body: Alignment, PMP, and translation-related checks can turn a memory op into an exception.

cards.title: Can memory serve it?
cards.body: Load and store OBI requests interact with the D-cache side of the memory system.

cards.title: May it change state?
cards.body: Store commit, no-store-pending, and commit-ready signals keep side effects disciplined.

content.takeaway: The LSU is a protocol hub, not a single-cycle arithmetic unit.

assets.caption: Official LSU block diagram from the CVA6 user manual archive.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: A helpful way to read the LSU is to ask four questions. Can it accept a new operation? Is the address legal and aligned? Can the cache or memory interface serve it? And for a store, may this operation actually change state? Those questions are more useful than memorizing every signal name. They also explain why LSU timing is often harder than ordinary integer execution.

actions.text: A helpful way to read the LSU is to ask four questions. Can it accept a new operation? Is the address legal and aligned? Can the cache or memory interface serve it? And for a store, may this operation actually change state? Those questions are more useful than memorizing every signal name. They also explain why LSU timing is often harder than ordinary integer execution.

content.title: Same address calculation, different architectural risk
content.subtitle: Loads and stores both use addresses, but they do not have the same side-effect profile.

columns.title: Load path
columns.body: A load can be issued toward memory once aliasing, translation, and cache interaction permit it. Its result and exception return to the backend.

columns.title: Store path
columns.body: A store may be tracked speculatively, but the memory write side effect is separated from the initial execution of the store instruction.

content.takeaway: Loads ask for a value; stores carry a side effect.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The load unit checks the store buffer before issuing a load and stalls load-after-store cases when the older store has not reached the cache.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Stores can be speculative, so outstanding stores are tracked separately from committed stores; commit turns an outstanding store into a committed one.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: This asymmetry is the key. Loads are still careful, because stale data and exceptions matter, but a load mainly asks memory for a value. Stores carry an architectural side effect. The design text says stores can be speculative and need commit before altering processor state. So never explain load and store as mirror images. That shortcut hides the point of the store buffer.

actions.text: This asymmetry is the key. Loads are still careful, because stale data and exceptions matter, but a load mainly asks memory for a value. Stores carry an architectural side effect. The design text says stores can be speculative and need commit before altering processor state. So never explain load and store as mirror images. That shortcut hides the point of the store buffer.

scenes.title: Memory can fail before data returns

content.title: The LSU must report more than values

items.title: Misalignment
items.body: CVA6 does not handle non-aligned memory accesses and raises misaligned exceptions for unsupported alignment.

items.title: Protection
items.body: PMP configuration reaches the LSU path and can constrain physical memory access.

items.title: Translation
items.body: When translation exists, page faults can convert a memory access into an exception.

items.title: Cache/interface response
items.body: The D-cache request and response path can stall or report conditions that shape backend progress.

content.takeaway: A memory instruction is not complete just because its address was computed.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: LSU overview
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy LSU note explains the DTLB, PTW, MMU, store buffer, load alias checks, and why loads can issue earlier than stores.

content.speakerNotes: A second habit: memory operations can fail before useful data returns. Misalignment, protection, translation, and cache-interface behavior all matter. The lesson here is not to memorize every exception code today. The lesson is to stop treating memory as an infinite array that always answers nicely. Hardware is more honest than that, and occasionally less polite.

actions.text: A second habit: memory operations can fail before useful data returns. Misalignment, protection, translation, and cache-interface behavior all matter. The lesson here is not to memorize every exception code today. The lesson is to stop treating memory as an infinite array that always answers nicely. Hardware is more honest than that, and occasionally less polite.

questions.question: Which description best fits the CVA6 LSU in this lesson?

options.label: A single-cycle ALU dedicated to address addition
A protocol hub for load/store operations, cache interaction, exceptions, and commit coordination

questions.analysis: The LSU connects issue, load/store result reporting, D-cache requests, PMP inputs, and commit-related store control.

questions.question: Which concerns belong in a careful LSU explanation?
questions.analysis: PC selection is a frontend topic; the other three are part of the memory-side LSU story.

questions.question: Why are stores more architecturally dangerous than loads?

options.label: Stores can change memory state and therefore need commit discipline

questions.analysis: A store carries a memory side effect, so speculative tracking and committed memory write must be separated.

questions.question: What is the safest statement about a memory operation after address calculation?

options.label: It may still stall, raise an exception, wait for translation, or wait for cache/interface response
It no longer needs instruction identity

questions.analysis: Address generation is only one part of memory execution; the memory path still has several checks and handshakes.

content.speakerNotes: This quiz checks whether the LSU map is in place. You should now see the LSU as a protocol hub: it accepts memory work, reports values or exceptions, checks store ordering, and coordinates with cache and commit. That map lets the next lesson zoom into the store buffer and load-store ordering without getting lost.

actions.text: This quiz checks whether the LSU map is in place. You should now see the LSU as a protocol hub: it accepts memory work, reports values or exceptions, checks store ordering, and coordinates with cache and commit. That map lets the next lesson zoom into the store buffer and load-store ordering without getting lost.

scenes.title: The LSU is the backend memory contract

content.title: Memory operations need contracts, not guesses
takeaways: The LSU bridges issue, execute, cache/interface, protection, and commit.
Loads and stores are asymmetric because stores carry side effects.
Misalignment, protection, translation, and cache response can shape memory progress.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: load/store queue discipline, store buffer states, alias checks, and why no forwarding matters.
content.closing: Once the LSU map is stable, memory ordering becomes explainable instead of mysterious.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A65X design: load_store_unit
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The LSU interfaces with the data cache, handles load/store operations, reports load and store results or exceptions, and connects to commit and PMP configuration.

content.speakerNotes: The summary is simple: the LSU is where backend memory work becomes real engineering. It bridges issue, execution, cache interaction, protection, and commit. Loads and stores are asymmetric. Exceptions and stalls are normal parts of the path. Next, we focus on the store buffer, load alias checks, and the queue discipline that keeps speculative memory from quietly corrupting reality.

actions.text: The summary is simple: the LSU is where backend memory work becomes real engineering. It bridges issue, execution, cache interaction, protection, and commit. Loads and stores are asymmetric. Exceptions and stalls are normal parts of the path. Next, we focus on the store buffer, load alias checks, and the queue discipline that keeps speculative memory from quietly corrupting reality.
